one_line_takeaway: FrameShield disentangles goal from framing in frozen LLM activations via a self-supervised contrastive/orthogonality/reconstruction objective (ReDAct), then flags jailbreaks as anomalies in the framing subspace, beating JBShield on most but not all of 8 LLM families on the authors' own GoalFrameBench and Chao et al. seed benchmarks.
key_finding: On GoalFrameBench harmful prompts (Table 2, Acc/F1), comparing against JBShield (the paper's stated closest prior weight-frozen representation-based detector) across 8 LLMs: FrameShield-Crit outperforms JBShield on all 8 models (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B: JBShield 0.66/0.68 vs FrameShield-Crit 0.94/0.93; Qwen3-4B: 0.70/0.74 vs 0.89/0.87), while FrameShield-Last improves over JBShield on most but not all models (it underperforms on Llama2-7B: 0.58/0.70 vs JBShield's 0.73/0.76). On the separate seed-prompt benchmark from Chao et al. 2025 (Table 3), FrameShield-Last is compared against LlamaGuard, SelfEx, GradSafe, and JBShield across 4 LLMs; FrameShield-Last attains the top accuracy on 3 of 4 models (Llama3-8B 0.96, Vicuna-7B 0.97, Mistral-7B 0.92, all beating JBShield's 0.77/0.91/0.88 respectively) but loses to JBShield on Qwen3-4B (0.84 vs JBShield's 0.87) and has lower F1 than JBShield in every column shown despite higher accuracy. A footnote in the paper notes critical-layer selection does NOT improve performance on Mistral and Llama3, attributed by the authors to convergence difficulties during ReDAct training on subsets of layers for those models. Separately, the layer-wise analysis (Section 6, Figure 5/6) shows goal and framing eta^2 signals peak at different depths and that ReDAct converges faster on layers closer to the output than layers closer to input, interpreted as evidence that semantic factors become more separable in deeper layers.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'goal-preserving framing' (GPF) jailbreak attacks (prompts that keep the harmful goal fixed but vary its linguistic framing to induce compliance) as a decision-theoretic composition X = S(goal, framing), then introduces a general self-supervised framework for disentangling any two semantic factors from a frozen LLM's hidden activations. The framework is instantiated in three concrete artifacts, all presented as the paper's own contributions: (1) GoalFrameBench, a corpus built by using GPT-4.1 to augment seed harmful/benign prompts from Chao et al.'s PAIR jailbreak set and JailbreakBench along two 'augmentation wheels' (vary goal / vary framing), yielding 5,286 generated prompts plus simple-goal 'null framing' prompts for a total of 6,269 prompts, producing 86,824 positive goal pairs and 89,419 positive framing pairs; (2) ReDAct (Representation Disentanglement on Activations), a two-headed MLP encoder/decoder attached to a frozen LLM layer, trained with a composite loss combining InfoNCE contrastive learning (sufficiency), an orthogonality penalty on <v_A, v_B> (controlled, non-zero leakage, not full independence), a reconstruction term (completeness), and gradient reversal; and (3) FrameShield, a Mahalanobis/whitened-residual anomaly detector applied only to the disentangled framing representation, with two variants (FrameShield-Last uses the final hidden layer; FrameShield-Crit selects a 'critical layer' via Cohen's d between benign/harmful residual scores, following the JBShield layer-selection procedure). The paper also proves an asymptotic sufficiency guarantee (Proposition 4.2: at the InfoNCE temperature limit, global minimizers achieve I(A;v_A*)=H(A) and I(B;v_B*)=H(B)) and a sample-complexity bound for pair coverage (Lemma C.1).
threat_model: Attacker crafts a prompt that preserves a fixed harmful goal G but manipulates its framing F (e.g., via PAIR-style iterative refinement, human rewrites, or model-generated paraphrases) to push the LLM's internal compliance score across its refusal threshold, without relying on token-level artifacts (suffixes, encodings) that structural filters can catch. The defense is representation-level, weight-frozen (no LLM fine-tuning, no backprop through the target model), and model-agnostic: it assumes white-box access to the target LLM's hidden activations at inference time but does not require retraining the LLM or knowledge of the attacker's specific goal.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation is that the framework 'currently focuses on binary semantic factor pairs' (only goal/framing), with extension to richer multi-factor decompositions left as future work; it also flags that 'end-to-end integrations that interact with model weights' were not explored (ReDAct only reads frozen activations). Beyond the authors' explicit caveats: the GoalFrameBench corpus itself is generated by prompting GPT-4.1 to create both goal and framing variants, so detection gains are partly measured against attacks whose distribution resembles the training corpus's own generative process; the full corpus is explicitly not released with the preprint, limiting reproducibility. Disentanglement is only partial by design (controlled, non-zero leakage is intentional, per Definition 4.3/Remark 4.4), and the paper reports eta^2 values that, while higher on-diagonal, remain modest in absolute terms (e.g., 0.22-0.37 range), indicating substantial residual entanglement. FrameShield-Last underperforms JBShield on at least one model in each of the two head-to-head tables (Llama2-7B in Table 2, Qwen3-4B accuracy and F1-in-general in Table 3), so the improvement is not uniform across all settings despite the paper's framing of consistent gains. F1 scores for FrameShield in Table 3 are lower than JBShield's on every listed model even where accuracy is higher, suggesting a precision/recall trade-off not discussed in the main text excerpt reviewed.

datasets_used: GoalFrameBench (paper's own corpus: 6,269 prompts total -- 5,286 generated via GPT-4.1 augmentation of goal/framing quadrants plus additional 'null framing' simple-goal prompts; yields 86,824 positive goal pairs and 89,419 positive framing pairs)
Seed jailbreak prompts from Chao et al. 2025 (PAIR)
Benign prompts from JailbreakBench (Chao et al. 2024; Mazeika et al. 2024; Zou et al. 2023; Mazeika et al. 2023)
quantitative_results: Table 1 (ANOVA eta^2, Llama-3-8B): Goal-vs-v_g=0.37, Goal-vs-v_f=0.18, Framing-vs-v_g=0.18, Framing-vs-v_f=0.22

Table 2 (GoalFrameBench, Acc/F1): FrameShield-Crit vs JBShield -- Qwen2.5-7B 0.94/0.93 vs 0.66/0.68; Qwen3-4B 0.89/0.87 vs 0.70/0.74; Llama2-7B 0.80/0.79 vs 0.73/0.76; Vicuna-13B 0.81/0.82 vs 0.63/0.67
Table 2: FrameShield-Last underperforms JBShield on Llama2-7B (0.58/0.70 vs 0.73/0.76)

Table 3 (Chao et al. 2025 seed prompts, Acc/F1): FrameShield-Last vs JBShield -- Llama3-8B 0.96/0.86 vs 0.77/0.86; Vicuna-7B 0.97/0.86 vs 0.91/0.91; Mistral-7B 0.92/0.64 vs 0.88/0.88; Qwen3-4B 0.84/0.68 vs 0.87/0.88 (FrameShield loses here)
Table 3: LlamaGuard, SelfEx, GradSafe baselines score lower than JBShield/FrameShield in most reported cells (e.g., GradSafe on Vicuna-7B: 0.03/0.06)
ReDAct training cost: a few hours on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU for 3 epochs over >86,000 pairs with a 7B-parameter LLM
FrameShield anomaly threshold set at the 95th percentile of a chi-squared reference distribution built from ~1200 benign framing representations, using PCA components explaining 80% of variance
baselines_compared: JBShield (Zhang et al. 2025) -- primary baseline, described by the authors as the state-of-the-art model-independent, weight-frozen jailbreak detector
